From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

National motto of Canada

This article is about Canada's national motto. For other uses of the phrase "From Sea to Sea", see [Sea to Sea (disambiguation)](/wiki/Sea_to_Sea_(disambiguation) "Sea to Sea (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Coat_of_arms_of_Canada_%28motto%29.svg/500px-Coat_of_arms_of_Canada_%28motto%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_Canada_(motto).svg)

The motto within the Canadian coat of arms

***A mari usque ad mare*** (Latin: [[aː ˈmariː ˈuːskʷɛ ad ˈmarɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *D'un océan à l'autre*, French pronunciation: [[dœ̃nɔseˈã aˈloʊ̯tʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); English: *From sea to sea*) is the [Canadian](/wiki/Canada "Canada") national [motto](/wiki/Motto "Motto"). The phrase comes from the [Latin Vulgate](/wiki/Latin_Vulgate "Latin Vulgate") translation of [Psalm 72](/wiki/Psalm_72 "Psalm 72"):8 in the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"):

> "*Et dominabitur a mari usque ad mare, et a flumine usque ad terminos terrae*"  
> ([King James Bible](/wiki/King_James_Bible "King James Bible"): "He shall have dominion also from sea to sea, and from the river unto the ends of the earth").[[1]](#cite_note-canenc1-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=A_mari_usque_ad_mare&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Coat_of_arms_of_Canada_%281921%29.jpg/250px-Coat_of_arms_of_Canada_%281921%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_Canada_(1921).jpg)

The [Arms of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada") as designed in 1921 with the national motto and original green maple leaves.

An early use of the phrase was by [George Monro Grant](/wiki/George_Monro_Grant "George Monro Grant"), who wrote a book called Ocean to Ocean about the geographic span of Canada,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and who was [Sandford Fleming](/wiki/Sandford_Fleming "Sandford Fleming")'s secretary and a [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") minister who used the phrase in his sermons. His great-grandson [Michael Ignatieff](/wiki/Michael_Ignatieff "Michael Ignatieff") suggests that Grant used the phrase in a nation-building effort during the construction of the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The use of the word "dominion" in the verse reflected the common use of the name "Dominion of Canada" for the new country.

The motto was first officially used in 1906 on the head of the mace of the new [Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan").[[1]](#cite_note-canenc1-1) This phrase was suggested for a national motto by [Joseph Pope](/wiki/Joseph_Pope_(public_servant) "Joseph Pope (public servant)"), then-Under Secretary of State, when the [Canadian coat of arms](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_Canada "Royal coat of arms of Canada") was redesigned in 1921.[[4]](#cite_note-description-4) Pope was a member of the four-person committee appointed by the federal government to redesign the coat of arms (the other members were Thomas Mulvey, A.G. Doughty and Major-General W.G. Gwatkin).[[5]](#cite_note-5) No motto had been included in the original design. Major-General W.G. Gwatkin proposed *in memoriam in spem* ("in memory, in hope") as a motto, but Pope's proposal garnered more support.[[1]](#cite_note-canenc1-1) The draft design was approved by Order in Council on April 21, 1921, and by the Royal Proclamation of King [George V](/wiki/George_V_of_the_United_Kingdom "George V of the United Kingdom") on November 21, 1921.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

As part of the Canadian coat of arms, the motto is used as a mark of authority by various government agencies and representatives.[[7]](#cite_note-parl1-7) It is also present on all denominations of [Canadian banknotes](/wiki/Banknotes_of_the_Canadian_dollar "Banknotes of the Canadian dollar"),[[8]](#cite_note-Bank1-8) and on the cover of [Canadian passports](/wiki/Canadian_passport "Canadian passport").[[9]](#cite_note-passport1-9) On its own, it appears on all federal government proclamations.[[10]](#cite_note-npiggy-10)

## Proposed amendment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=A_mari_usque_ad_mare&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Proposed amendment")]

See also: [Arctic policy of Canada](/wiki/Arctic_policy_of_Canada "Arctic policy of Canada")

In March 2007, the premiers of Canada's [three territories](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada#Territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") called for the amendment of the motto to reflect the vast geographic nature of Canada's territory,[[11]](#cite_note-star1-11) as Canada has coastlines on the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean"), [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), and [Pacific Oceans](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). Two suggestions for a new motto are *A mari ad mare ad mare* (from sea to sea to sea) and *A mari usque ad maria* (from the sea to the other seas).[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The expanded informal version of the motto ("from sea to sea to sea") is used in speeches and writings about Canada, representing inclusiveness toward northern residents and the growing significance of the Arctic in Canada's political and economic future.[[10]](#cite_note-npiggy-10) A [Canwest Global](/wiki/Canwest_Global "Canwest Global")-commissioned poll showed proponents of amending the motto outnumbering opponents in the ratio of three to one, with one-third of those polled neutral.[[10]](#cite_note-npiggy-10)
